title: Stories (Ephemeral Content) — System Design
description: Design ephemeral stories: short-lived posts (photo/video) that appear in followers' story trays and auto-vanish after 24 hours. The themes are TTL-driven expiry everywhere, fanout to a per-user tray of unexpired stories, per-viewer seen-state, and cheap automatic cleanup at scale.

nodes.label: Mobile Client
nodes.description: Posts stories, views followees' story trays, marks them seen.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.description: Routes post, tray, and view requests.

nodes.label: Story Post Service
nodes.description: Accepts a story, stores media, sets TTL, fans out to followers.

nodes.label: Story Store
nodes.description: Story metadata with expiry, sharded by author; auto-purged.

nodes.label: Social Graph
nodes.description: Follower edges for fanout (who sees whose stories).

nodes.label: Fanout Service
nodes.description: Pushes a new story id into each follower's story tray.

nodes.label: Story Tray Cache
nodes.description: Per-user list of unexpired stories from followees (with TTL).

nodes.label: Seen-State Store
nodes.description: Which stories a user has already viewed (per viewer).

nodes.label: Expiry Reaper
nodes.description: Purges expired stories from stores/trays past their TTL.

nodes.label: View Event Stream
nodes.description: View events for seen-state, view counts, and analytics.

functional: Post a story (photo/video) visible to followers for a limited time (e.g. 24h).
View followees' active stories as a tray, ordered (often unseen-first).
Track which stories a viewer has already seen.
Show the author who viewed their story (viewer list / counts).
Automatically remove stories when they expire.
nonFunctional: Everything is ephemeral — expiry must be reliable and cheap (no expired story ever shown).
Read-heavy: viewing trays vastly outnumbers posting.
Low-latency tray reads and media playback (CDN).
Per-viewer seen-state at scale (each viewer x each story).
Scale to large follower counts; cleanup must not be a manual or expensive sweep.

capacity.label: Lifespan
capacity.note: Then gone — bounds storage and tray size.

api.description: Post a story; stores media with TTL and fans out to followers' trays.
Get the viewer's story tray (active stories from followees, unseen-first).
Record a view (marks seen; counts toward the author's viewer list).

dataModel.name: stories
dataModel.note: Story metadata with an expiry; auto-purged, sharded by author.

dataModel.name: story_tray (cache)
dataModel.note: Per-user list of unexpired story ids from followees, with TTL entries.

dataModel.name: seen_state (cache)
dataModel.note: Which stories a viewer has seen (ephemeral, TTL-aligned).

patterns.name: TTL-native storage
patterns.what: Tag every story/media/tray entry with an expiry so the store auto-purges it (no manual delete).
patterns.whenToUse: Content that is meant to disappear on a deadline.

patterns.name: Fanout-on-write to trays
patterns.what: Push a new story id into each follower's tray at post time for cheap reads.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy ephemeral feeds with bounded fanout.

patterns.name: Hybrid fanout (celebrity pull)
patterns.what: Pull stories of very-high-follower authors at read time instead of pushing.

patterns.name: Per-viewer seen-state
patterns.what: Track seen stories per viewer to order unseen-first and dim seen ones.
patterns.whenToUse: Story UIs that distinguish new vs viewed.

patterns.name: Lazy + TTL expiry
patterns.what: Rely on store TTL for cleanup plus filter expired entries on read; a reaper backstops trays.

patterns.name: Async view counting
patterns.what: Aggregate view events for the author's viewer list/counts off the hot path.

steps.title: 1. Ephemeral by Design: TTL Everywhere
description: The defining property is that stories vanish after a deadline. So make expiry intrinsic, not a bolted-on cleanup job: every artifact — the story record, its media, and tray entries — carries an expiresAt and is stored in TTL-native stores that auto-purge.

A story is posted with media stored in an object store tagged with a lifecycle/TTL rule, and a story record whose store auto-expires rows past expires_at. Nothing requires a scan-and-delete job to enumerate and remove old content.
This 'TTL-native' choice is the foundation: it makes cleanup cheap and automatic, which is essential when hundreds of millions of stories are created and must all disappear daily.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you delete hundreds of millions of expired stories a day without an expensive sweep job?

concepts.definition: Every artifact carries an expiry; stores auto-purge past it (object lifecycle rules, DB TTL).
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes ephemeral cleanup automatic and cheap instead of a giant scheduled delete.
concepts.example: Story row expires at created+24h; media lifecycle rule deletes the blob.

whyNow: TTL-native storage is the foundational decision for ephemeral content; it's what makes the whole 'disappears at scale' requirement tractable.

recap.newRisk: Followers need to see new stories fast — a read-heavy fanout problem like any feed.

steps.title: 2. Fanout to Story Trays
description: Viewing is read-heavy, so precompute each user's story tray: when someone posts, fan out the story id into each follower's tray cache (fanout-on-write), so reading the tray is a cheap cached lookup, not a query across all followees.

Tray entries themselves carry the story's TTL, so the tray naturally only contains active stories. As with any feed, the heavy-tailed follower distribution means celebrities are handled by pull (fetch their active stories at read time) rather than pushing to tens of millions of trays — a hybrid fanout.
The tray stores story ids (and authors); media is hydrated from the CDN on view. This is the same fanout model as a permanent feed, but every entry is short-lived.
steps.decisionPrompt: A user with 50M followers posts a story. Do you push it into 50M trays?

concepts.definition: Push a story id into followers' trays at post time; entries expire with the story.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cheap tray reads, and the tray self-cleans as entries expire.
concepts.example: Push to normal followers' trays; pull celebrities at read time.

whyNow: Fanout is the read-scaling mechanism; reusing the push/pull hybrid with TTL'd entries connects it to feeds while staying ephemeral.

recap.after: Per-user trays of active stories via hybrid fanout.
recap.newRisk: Users want to know which stories are new — per-viewer seen-state.

steps.title: 3. Per-Viewer Seen-State
description: Story UIs distinguish unseen (bright ring, shown first) from already-viewed. That requires tracking, per viewer, which stories they've seen — a viewer x story relationship that's large but, helpfully, also ephemeral (it only matters while the story is alive).

Keep seen-state in a fast store keyed by viewer (a set of seen story ids), aligned to the story TTL so it auto-expires too. Reading the tray filters/orders by this set (unseen-first); viewing a story adds it to the set.
Because seen-state expires with the stories, it never accumulates unbounded — the ephemerality that defines stories also bounds this otherwise-explosive relationship.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you track which of a user's many stories each of their many followers has seen, without it growing forever?

concepts.definition: Per-viewer set of seen story ids, TTL-aligned to the stories so it self-bounds.
concepts.whyItMatters: Enables unseen-first UX without an ever-growing viewer x story table.
concepts.example: seen[viewer] = {s_9, s_42}; expires as those stories do.

whyNow: Seen-state is the story-specific UX requirement; recognizing it's bounded by the same TTL is the elegant insight.

recap.newRisk: Even with TTLs, expired entries can linger in caches — reads must never show an expired story.

steps.title: 4. Reliable Expiry (Never Show a Stale Story)
description: TTL stores purge eventually, but purge timing is approximate — an expired story id could still sit in a tray cache for a bit. The hard guarantee is that no expired story is ever shown, so combine mechanisms: filter on read (drop any tray entry whose expiresAt has passed) as the always-correct safety net, plus TTL auto-purge for the bulk cleanup.
A lightweight reaper backstops trays (drop expired ids) and reconciles any straggler media, but it's a cheap pass over recently-touched data, not a global scan — the TTL stores do the heavy lifting.
Layering read-time filtering over TTL purge gives both correctness (read filter) and cheap cleanup (TTL) — the key to ephemeral correctness at scale.
steps.decisionPrompt: A story expired 30 seconds ago but its id is still in some trays. How do you guarantee no one sees it?

concepts.definition: Always drop expired entries at read time; rely on store TTL for bulk cleanup; a reaper backstops.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees correctness (read filter) while keeping cleanup cheap (TTL).
concepts.example: Tray read skips entries past expiresAt even before TTL purge runs.

whyNow: Reliable expiry is the correctness crux of ephemeral content; filter-on-read over TTL purge is the layered answer that never shows a stale story.

recap.before: Approximate purge could leak a stale story.
recap.after: Read-time filtering guarantees no expired story shown.
recap.newRisk: Authors want to know who viewed their story — high-volume view tracking.

traps.trap: Relying only on a scheduled deletion job to remove expired stories.
traps.why: Scanning hundreds of millions daily is expensive, and any lag risks showing expired content.
traps.instead: TTL-native stores + filter-on-read; reaper only as a cheap backstop.

steps.title: 5. View Counts and Viewer Lists
description: Authors see who viewed their story (a viewer list) and a count. Views are high-volume; recording each synchronously to the story record would hotspot popular authors. Emit view events to a stream; an aggregator builds the per-story viewer list and count.
View events also drive seen-state (the viewer has now seen it). The viewer list is itself ephemeral — it only matters while the story is alive — so it expires with the story, again bounding the data.
Counts can be approximate for very popular stories; the viewer list is capped/paginated. The stream decouples view capture from these aggregations and from the hot read path.
steps.decisionPrompt: A celebrity's story gets 10M views. Do you write each view to the story row?
whyNow: View tracking is a core story feature and a high-volume counter; async aggregation off the hot path is the standard scaling answer.

concepts.definition: Aggregate view events into per-story (TTL'd) viewer lists/counts off the hot path.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids hotspotting popular stories; the data self-bounds with the story.
concepts.example: View events -> aggregate -> viewer list (expires with the story).

recap.newRisk: Posting spikes, big fanouts, and tray reads must scale; nothing should linger.

steps.title: 6. Scaling and Cleanup at Volume
description: Shard stories by author and trays/seen-state by viewer; replicate tray caches for read volume. Media is CDN-served, absorbing view fanout. Fanout bursts (a popular account posts) buffer in a queue with scalable workers; celebrity authors use the pull path to avoid mega-fanout.

The ephemerality is a scaling gift: total live data is bounded to ~24h of stories, so stores never grow unbounded and TTL purge keeps them lean automatically — no ballooning storage, no giant delete jobs.

Treat trays, seen-state, and viewer lists as rebuildable derived state (rebuild a tray from followees' active stories on cache loss). The story store + media are the authoritative, self-expiring truth.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why does the 24h ephemerality actually make this system EASIER to scale than a permanent feed?
whyNow: Closing on sharding + bounded live data shows the ephemerality is a scaling advantage and the derived stores are rebuildable — completing the design.

recap.newRisk: Fanout lag on viral posts and cache rebuilds — bounded by queueing, pull, and rebuildable derived state.

bottlenecks.issue: Big fanout on a popular post.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Queue + scalable fanout workers; pull for celebrity authors.

failureDrills.scenario: A user's tray cache is evicted.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Rebuild it by pulling followees' currently-active stories; only unexpired ones appear.
failureDrills.mitigation: Tray as rebuildable derived state over the authoritative, self-expiring story store.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Ephemeral Stories
finalDesign.description: Stories are TTL-native end to end: media (object lifecycle rule) and story records (DB TTL) auto-expire, so cleanup is automatic and cheap. Posting fans out a story id into followers' tray caches (hybrid: pull for celebrities), with tray entries carrying the story's TTL. Reads serve the tray with per-viewer seen-state (unseen-first) and hydrate media from CDN, always filtering expired entries on read so no stale story is ever shown (a reaper is only a cheap backstop). View events stream to async aggregation for ephemeral viewer lists/counts. Stores shard by author/viewer; total live data is bounded to ~24h; trays/seen-state/viewer-lists are rebuildable derived state over the self-expiring authoritative story store.

nonFunctional.how: ~24h live data + sharding + rebuildable derived state.

say: Confirm ephemeral: stories disappear after ~24h, read-heavy trays.
Pin reliable+cheap expiry and never-show-expired as the goals.
Note seen-state per viewer and the heavy-tailed follower distribution.
Fanout into per-user trays (hybrid for celebrities).
Scaling: bounded live data, sharding, rebuildable trays.
Tradeoffs: push vs pull threshold, purge lag vs read-filter, view-count accuracy.
Mention close-friends/visibility and replies as extensions.

expectations: Stores stories with a TTL and fans out to trays.
May rely on a delete job and miss read-filtering/seen-state.
Reasons that ephemerality bounds data + seen-state.
Handles celebrity fanout and rebuildable trays at scale.

followUps: How do you guarantee an expired story is never shown even before TTL purge runs?
How would you support visibility controls (close friends, hidden-from lists)?
How do you bound per-viewer seen-state from growing unbounded?
How would you handle replies/reactions to a story (DMs)?
Why is an ephemeral feed easier to scale than a permanent one?
